title: Launch-time Accessibility permission re-grant modal (dev cert reset)
description:
Bringr is built as an unsigned / ad-hoc-signed dev build, so every rebuild produces a fresh code signature. macOS TCC treats each new signature as a different app and revokes the previously granted Accessibility permission. The practical result: on nearly every launch during development `AXIsProcessTrusted()` returns false, so Bringr cannot enumerate/control windows (US-003/US-004) or install its global event tap (US-007) — i.e. it cannot run normally. This blocks day-to-day dev iteration, hence P0.

Today `AppDelegate.applicationDidFinishLaunching` only fires a bare system prompt (`permissions.requestAccess()`). macOS shows that prompt reliably only once per app identity and it offers no re-check step or guidance, which is poor for the repeated re-grant loop we hit on every rebuild.

Port ClipSlop's launch-time permission alert modal. Reference implementation in ~/Projects/ClipSlop:
- Sources/Views/PermissionAlert/PermissionAlertWindow.swift (floating NSWindow, `moveAside()` slides it left so it does not cover the system dialog)
- Sources/Views/PermissionAlert/PermissionAlertView.swift (per-permission Grant -> Validate flow, numbered manual instructions, "don't show again" toggle, Close)
- Sources/App/AppState.swift: `checkPermissionsAfterUpdate()` / `showPermissionAlert()` / `movePermissionAlertAside()` / `dismissPermissionAlert()`

Bringr is Accessibility-only (no Screen Recording), so build the single-permission version — drop ClipSlop's screen-recording card.

What to build:
1. A dedicated launch-time alert window, separate from the existing Preferences pane (Bringr/PreferencesView.swift), that appears when `!permissions.isTrusted` at launch.
2. Reuse the existing `PermissionsManager` (Bringr/PermissionsManager.swift). Do NOT introduce ClipSlop's static `PermissionService` enum — Bringr's manager already has `requestAccess()`, `openAccessibilitySettings()`, `recheck()`, `status`/`PermissionStatus` copy, and `startMonitoring()` (1s poll + `com.apple.accessibility.api` DistributedNotificationCenter observer).
3. Actions in the modal: a "Grant Accessibility Access" button that fires the system prompt and slides the window aside (ClipSlop's `moveAside()`) so it does not cover the system dialog; an "Open System Settings" button (`openAccessibilitySettings()`); and numbered manual steps (System Settings -> Privacy & Security -> Accessibility -> enable Bringr), reusing the copy already in `PermissionStatus.detail`.
4. Auto-dismiss: because `PermissionsManager` already monitors live, the modal should observe `isTrusted` and close itself the instant access is granted (~1s), with no relaunch. This is the key win for the constant re-grant cycle and improves on ClipSlop's manual Validate button.
5. Wire into `AppDelegate.applicationDidFinishLaunching`: keep the existing XCTest guard, call `startMonitoring()`, and if `!isTrusted` present this modal instead of the bare `requestAccess()` prompt.

Menu-bar app note (LSUIElement = true): the window must use a floating level and `NSApp.activate(ignoringOtherApps:)` to come to front, like ClipSlop's PermissionAlertWindow. Bringr currently uses SwiftUI `Window` scenes (about/preferences) opened via `openWindow(id:)`; an AppKit `NSWindow` subclass is recommended here for full control over floating level + `moveAside()` + transparent titlebar (see Design notes).

Optional "Don't show again" toggle: Bringr has no persisted settings yet; this would add the first `@AppStorage("suppressPermissionAlert")` bool. Nice-to-have, default OFF — a developer who rebuilds constantly probably wants the reminder, so do not block the core flow on it.
design:
- Reference: ClipSlop ~/Projects/ClipSlop/Sources/Views/PermissionAlert/{PermissionAlertWindow,PermissionAlertView}.swift and Sources/App/AppState.swift (checkPermissionsAfterUpdate, showPermissionAlert, movePermissionAlertAside, dismissPermissionAlert).
- Reuse Bringr's PermissionsManager + PermissionStatus copy; do NOT port ClipSlop's static PermissionService enum.
- Single permission only (Accessibility) — remove ClipSlop's Screen Recording card and its live-check branch.
- Window: prefer an AppKit NSWindow subclass (level = .floating, isReleasedWhenClosed = false, transparent titlebar, center, moveAside()) over a SwiftUI Window scene, for full control of the floating level and the move-aside-on-prompt behavior in a menu-bar-only (LSUIElement) app. Owner/coordinator can live on AppDelegate, mirroring ClipSlop's AppState ownership.
- Prefer auto-dismiss by observing PermissionsManager.isTrusted over ClipSlop's manual Validate button — Bringr already monitors permission state live (startMonitoring), which ClipSlop's static enum did not.
- Extends the already-closed US-002 (Bringr-93j.2, Accessibility permission bootstrap); this is the dev-experience hardening of that bootstrap, not a PRD user story.
acceptance_criteria:
- On launch, when Accessibility is NOT granted (and not suppressed), the permission modal appears; when it IS granted, the modal does not appear.
- The modal offers a "Grant Accessibility Access" action (system prompt) and an "Open System Settings" action, plus numbered manual steps for the Accessibility pane.
- Firing the system prompt slides/moves the modal aside so it does not cover the system dialog (ClipSlop moveAside parity).
- When the user grants access in System Settings, the modal auto-dismisses within ~1s with no relaunch, driven by PermissionsManager's existing monitoring (poll + accessibility-changed notification).
- The bare `permissions.requestAccess()` call at launch in AppDelegate.applicationDidFinishLaunching is replaced by the modal flow; the XCTest guard remains so test runs never pop a window or system prompt.
- Unit test (extend BringrTests/PermissionsManagerTests.swift): the launch-decision logic presents the modal when the injected probe returns false and does not when it returns true, using the existing dependency-injection pattern.
- App builds clean: `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends with `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`.

title: US-001: Stand up lint and test tooling
description:
As a developer, I want SwiftLint and an XCTest target wired into the project so that the quality gates can run from story one.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A unit-test target is added to `Bringr.xcodeproj` and contains at least one passing sample test.
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` runs and passes.
- [ ] SwiftLint is integrated (build plugin or invoked binary) with a committed `.swiftlint.yml`.
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` runs clean against the current source.
- [ ] The three quality-gate commands are documented in the README or CLAUDE.md.

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests

title: US-002: Accessibility permission bootstrap
description:
As a user, I want Bringr to detect and request Accessibility/Input-Monitoring permission so that it can enumerate and control windows and observe global input.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] On launch, the app checks `AXIsProcessTrusted()` and reflects the current state.
- [ ] If untrusted, the app prompts the user and links to the relevant System Settings pane.
- [ ] Preferences shows current permission status and a "re-check" action.
- [ ] When permission is missing, activation features degrade gracefully (no crash) and surface a clear message.
- [ ] Permission state changes are picked up without requiring a manual relaunch where the OS allows it.

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests
- [ ] Build and run (`pkill -x Bringr 2>/dev/null; open build/Build/Products/Debug/Bringr.app`) and verify the behavior visually

title: US-012 follow-up: previous active app stays above selected app
description: Selecting a window from the radial menu still leaves the app that was frontmost before summon above the chosen app in the global z-order. Need to make commit restore and activation ordering ensure the selected app/window becomes truly frontmost after restore/unhide races.
notes: New reproduction clue from user: selecting/releasing on a second-level window circle works, but selecting/releasing on the first-level app circle leaves the previously active app above the chosen app. Focus fix should target app-level commit path and ensure choosing an app activates/raises one of its windows, not only restores reveal state.
close_reason: Implemented app-slice commit so releasing on the first-level app circle restores reveal state and activates/focuses the selected app's current front window; added regression coverage and verified lint/tests/build/run.

title: US-012 follow-up: selected window keeps losing focus
description: Committing a selected window from the radial menu still often leaves the originally front sibling window in front/focused after release. Strengthen the commit/focus path so the chosen window reliably wins after restore, activation, AX focus, and raise.
close_reason: Adjusted commit focus to AutoRaise-style AX raise before app activation, strengthened app activation, and verified lint/tests/build/launch

title: US-012 follow-up: committed window does not rise or take focus
description:
Reported against US-012 (Bringr-93j.12, closed): committing a window from the wheel does not bring it forward. The chosen window neither rises to the front nor takes keyboard focus, so the core "select and commit" flow is effectively non-functional.

## Symptom
Clicking/releasing on a window slice runs the commit path, but the target window stays behind the current frontmost app — it does not rise and does not become key/main.

## Commit path
RadialMenuWindow.commitSelection -> RadialNavigator.commit -> WindowControl.commit(_:) (Bringr/WindowControl.swift:117) -> raiseAndFocus(_:) (line 101), which calls:
- system.activate(window.app)  -> NSRunningApplication.activate() (line 268)
- system.raise(window)         -> AXUIElementPerformAction(kAXRaiseAction) (line 282)
- system.focusWindow(window)   -> kAXMain + kAXFocused (line 287)

## Suspected causes to investigate
- Silent no-op on stale/missing AX element: every live primitive does `guard let element = elementCache[window] else { return }` (lines 283, 288). If the hide/restore cycle invalidates or fails to repopulate elementCache for the chosen window, raise/focus do nothing and fail silently.
- Activation ordering/race: commit() runs restore() first, and restore() re-activates the prior frontmost app last (line 188) before raiseAndFocus runs. NSRunningApplication.activate() is async, so the prior app may win the race and stay frontmost.
- App activation semantics: plain NSRunningApplication.activate() (no options) may not reliably bring the app forward on macOS 14+; kAXRaiseAction only reorders within the app's own window layer, so without the app actually activating, the window rises behind the frontmost app.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Committing a window reliably raises it to the front and moves keyboard focus to it (key + main), for apps with one or many windows.
- [ ] Works whether the chosen window was previously normal, minimized, or behind other apps.
- [ ] A regression test covers the commit -> restore -> raise/focus ordering so a silent cache-miss or activation race is caught.
- [ ] Verified by build & run: summon, pick a window of a backgrounded app, confirm it comes to front and is focused.

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests
- [ ] Build and run (`pkill -x Bringr 2>/dev/null; open build/Build/Products/Debug/Bringr.app`) and verify the behavior visually

title: US-012: Select and commit — focus and remember
description:
As a user, I want clicking/releasing on a window to bring it forward and have Bringr remember my choice so that the next summon starts from where I left off.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Committing a window raises it, activates its app, and moves focus to it.
- [ ] All previously hidden apps/windows are restored to their pre-summon state after commit.
- [ ] The chosen window is persisted as the last selection for that app (best-effort match by app + window title/order across restarts).
- [ ] On the next summon, the remembered window for an app is pre-highlighted when present.
- [ ] Persistence and pre-highlight selection logic are unit-tested.

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests
- [ ] Build and run (`pkill -x Bringr 2>/dev/null; open build/Build/Products/Debug/Bringr.app`) and verify the behavior visually

title: US-006: Overlay window and radial layout
description:
As a user, I want the wheel to appear instantly at my cursor with evenly placed slices so that interaction is immediate and predictable.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A borderless, transparent, floating, click-through-safe overlay window is pre-warmed at launch and shown/hidden rather than recreated.
- [ ] Given N items, slices are laid out evenly around the ring with computed geometry.
- [ ] Hit-testing maps a cursor position to the correct slice (or "none" in the dead zone/outside).
- [ ] v1 slice content is placeholder: app icon/name for app slices; index number and/or title for window slices (no live preview).
- [ ] The overlay appears on the display under the cursor and is positioned at the cursor.
- [ ] No animations. Slice geometry and hit-testing are unit-tested; appearance is verified by build & run.

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests
- [ ] Build and run (`pkill -x Bringr 2>/dev/null; open build/Build/Products/Debug/Bringr.app`) and verify the behavior visually

title: US-004: Window control primitives
description:
As the menu, I want primitives to raise/focus a window, hide and restore other apps, and hide and restore other windows of one app so that reveal strategies and selection can be built on them.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Can raise a specific window and move focus to it (target app activated, window made main/front).
- [ ] Can hide all apps except a target app, and restore them to their prior state.
- [ ] Can hide all windows of an app except a target window, and restore them.
- [ ] Captures prior z-order/visibility before mutating so it can be restored exactly.
- [ ] A restore call after any sequence returns windows/apps to their pre-summon visibility and ordering.

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests
- [ ] Build and run (`pkill -x Bringr 2>/dev/null; open build/Build/Products/Debug/Bringr.app`) and verify the behavior visually

title: US-005: Nestable, non-singleton menu model
description:
As a developer, I want a tree-based menu model with dynamic node providers so that future menus, deeper nesting, and new action types are additive rather than rewrites.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A `MenuNode` tree supports child nodes to arbitrary depth.
- [ ] Node content can be static or supplied by a dynamic provider (e.g. "running apps", "windows of app X").
- [ ] A node carries an action (v1 actions: expand-to-children, focus-window); the action set is open for extension.
- [ ] No global/singleton menu instance — a menu is an instantiable definition resolved by a registry keyed on its trigger.
- [ ] Unit tests build the apps→windows tree from enumeration fixtures and assert structure.

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests

title: US-003: Window enumeration service
description:
As the menu, I want a service that reports which apps currently have on-screen windows and the windows belonging to each so that the wheel reflects live state at summon time.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Returns running apps that own at least one normal, on-screen window.
- [ ] Returns per-app window list with a stable identifier, title, and owning app.
- [ ] Excludes Bringr itself and apps with no normal windows (e.g. agents, menu-bar-only apps).
- [ ] Enumeration is invoked fresh on each summon and completes fast enough not to delay the menu (measure and record the timing).
- [ ] Logic is covered by unit tests using injected fixtures (no live system dependency in tests).

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests

title: Radial Window Switcher (Bringr v1)
description:
Bringr's single job in v1 is to switch fast between multiple windows of the same app without touching the keyboard. The user summons a radial (pie) menu at the cursor; it shows the apps that currently have open windows; hovering an app reveals a sub-wheel of that app's windows; hovering a window isolates it on screen for visual confirmation; clicking brings it forward and focuses it. The whole flow stays in the wheel — no Cmd-Tab, Mission Control, or Dock guessing.

This document covers v1 only. URLs, files, folders, custom commands, real window previews, user-created menus, and animations are explicitly future work, but the architecture must not foreclose them.

Source PRD: ./tasks/prd-radial-window-switcher-bringr-v1.md

title: Enabling "Include minimized windows" / "Include hidden apps" destroys pie-menu performance — needs a performance-focused rework
description:
When the collection settings "Include minimized windows" or "Include hidden apps" are turned on, performance is completely destroyed. The pie menu becomes basically unusable — every action lags by a couple of seconds. It's terrible.

This whole collection path needs to be reworked from the ground up with a strong focus on performance: gathering minimized and hidden windows/apps must not slow the menu down. Opening the pie, hovering, and selecting should stay fast and responsive even with these options enabled.

Related to the collection settings in Bringr-93j.48 and Bringr-93j.50.

title: "Include windows from all screens / all Spaces" surface phantom windows that don't really exist
description:
The collection options from Bringr-93j.48 — "Include windows from all screens" and "Include windows from all Spaces" — are adding windows that don't actually exist. They show up as options in the pie, but I can't see any real window for them, and when I select one I still just end up on the app's main window. So these are phantom / ghost entries, not real windows.

What I'm seeing:
- Google Chrome: gets these weird ghost windows with BOTH checkboxes on ("all screens" and "all Spaces").
- Ghostty terminal: gets ghost windows only when "Include windows from all Spaces" is on.

Please investigate where these phantom windows are coming from — it may be some kind of window/element being mis-collected — and stop them from appearing. Only real, selectable windows should show up in the pie.

Medium priority.

title: Collect only real Dock apps — exclude hidden background/menu-bar apps from all-spaces/all-screens collection
description:
Refinement related to Bringr-93j.48 (configurable app/window collection across screens and spaces). The "include apps from all Spaces" (and all-screens) option currently also pulls in apps that are hidden in the status bar / menu bar and have no Dock presence — background and agent apps that run without a Dock icon. When we collect across all screens like we do now, that becomes a problem: the pie fills up with background apps we never wanted.

Instead, when collecting apps, only take the apps that are actually in the Dock. The ones we want are:
- Apps that appear in the Dock (a real Dock instance — not background / agent / menu-bar-only apps).
- That are running.
- That have at least one window (minimized or not — doesn't matter).

We still need to support multiple spaces and multiple screens — that part stays. The change is just to stop including the hidden, no-Dock, background apps; come up with a reliable way to scan for the genuine Dock apps only.

title: Add settings to include minimized and hidden windows when collecting windows
description:
An adjustment related to Bringr-93j.48 (configurable app/window collection across screens and spaces). On top of the screen and space options, we also want ways to collect windows that aren't normally visible.

Add new checkboxes in the collection settings:
- Include minimized windows — when on, currently minimized windows are gathered and show up as options in the pie; when off, they're left out (as today).
- Include hidden windows — when on, windows belonging to apps that have been hidden (e.g. via Hide / Cmd-H, including apps hidden by Bringr's own "hide everything else" behaviour) are gathered and show up in the pie; when off, they're left out (as today).

Background on the hidden case: right now, if an app's window was hidden (not minimized — actually hidden), it isn't collected the next time you open the pie menu, so it effectively disappears from the switcher. The "include hidden windows" checkbox is what brings those back into the collection. See Bringr-93j.49, whose "hide other apps" behaviour can trigger exactly this situation.

These sit alongside the other window-collection settings.

title: Make app/window collection across screens and spaces configurable in Preferences
description:
Earlier we made the menu collect apps and windows only from the screen where it was opened (see Bringr-93j.30). Rather than that being fixed, let's make this collection behaviour configurable with new settings in Preferences.

There are two categories of settings — one for how apps are collected, and one for how a given app's windows are collected. Each category controls two independent things: which screens to pull from, and which spaces (virtual desktops) to pull from.

Apps (which apps appear in the first-level pie):
- A checkbox to include apps from all spaces (virtual desktops), versus only the current space.
- A checkbox to include apps from all screens, versus only the current screen.

Windows (which of an app's windows appear in the second-level pie):
- A checkbox to include that app's windows from all screens, versus only the current screen.
- A checkbox to include its windows from all spaces, versus only the current space.

In short: let me independently decide, for both apps and windows, whether collection is limited to the current screen/space or spans all screens and all spaces.

title: My Apps: Show all other running apps toggle
description: Add a Show all other running apps toggle. When ON, append the remaining running apps (those with on-screen windows and not already in the curated list) after the pinned curated block. When OFF, show only the curated apps. Default ON so an empty list reproduces the current full wheel and existing users see no regression. The on a certain screen part: the appended apps should be scoped to the screen the menu was opened on, which is delivered by the multi-monitor task Bringr-93j.30, hence the dependency. Surface the toggle in Preferences and implement the append in the wheel-composition layer.
acceptance_criteria: Toggle ON appends non-listed running apps after the list; OFF shows only the list; appended apps respect the active screen once Bringr-93j.30 lands.

title: My Apps: Do not sort my custom list checkbox
description: Add a Do not sort my custom list checkbox to the Sorting section. When ON, the curated apps keep their manual order regardless of the active Apps sort order (AppSortOrder), and only the non-listed other apps are sorted by it. When OFF, the active Apps sort order may reorder the listed apps too. Implement where sorting is applied (WindowEnumerator.sorted or the wheel-composition layer) and surface the checkbox in the Sorting section beside the existing Apps and Windows pickers. Persist via UserDefaults like AppSortOrder.
acceptance_criteria: With a sort active and the box ON, listed apps stay in manual order while other apps sort; with the box OFF, the sort applies to the whole ring.

title: My Apps: build the top-level wheel from the curated list
description: Compose the top-level ring from the curated list rather than only the raw enumeration. For each listed app: if it is running with on-screen windows, produce the current expand-to-windows app node (resolve bundle id to running pid to AppWindows from WindowEnumerator); if it is not running or has no windows, produce a launch node (from the launch-action task) carrying the bundle id and icon (from the icon task). Pin the listed apps in the user manual order at the front of the ring. This is the join point of the model, icon, and launch tasks, likely a composition layer over WindowEnumerator and WindowSwitcherMenu. An empty list falls through to current behavior.
acceptance_criteria: With a list of one running and one not-running app, the wheel shows both in list order; the running one expands to its windows, the not-running one launches on select; an empty list reproduces the current wheel.

title: My Apps: Preferences list editor (picker + drag, remove, reorder, icons)
description: Add a My Apps section to PreferencesView bound to the curated-list model (from the persistence task). Show a list of rows, each with the app icon (NSWorkspace.icon(forFile:)) and display name. Controls: add via a native Open-panel scoped to applications (NSOpenPanel with allowedContentTypes of .application, defaulting to /Applications); add via drag-and-drop of app bundles from Finder or the Dock; remove with a minus control; reorder by dragging and persist that manual order. Follow the existing section(_:) and @AppStorage conventions; the Preferences window is already scrollable. The manual order set here is the default wheel order for listed apps.
acceptance_criteria: An app can be added via the picker and via drag; rows show icon and name; remove and reorder persist across relaunch and match the order the wheel uses.

title: My Apps: launch a listed app when selected and not running
description: A listed app that is not running, or has no on-screen windows, should start when chosen in the wheel. Add a MenuAction case to launch by bundle identifier and execute it via NSWorkspace.shared.openApplication(at:configuration:), resolving the URL from the bundle id. Wire it through the commit path in RadialNavigator, which today handles .focusWindow and the app-level commit(_ app:). When the listed app is running with windows, keep current behavior (expand or focus); this task adds only the not-running launch branch. Put the launch behind a small seam (mirroring WindowControlling) so it stays unit-testable. Depends on the icon-rendering task for the bundle-id-bearing menu node.
acceptance_criteria: Selecting a listed, not-running app launches it; selecting a running listed app still focuses or expands; the launch path is testable behind a seam.

title: My Apps: render slice icons for apps that are not running
description: App slices currently take their icon from NSRunningApplication(pid).icon (RadialMenuView around line 138) and AppID is pid-only. A curated app may not be running, so the wheel must render its icon from the bundle on disk. Design steer: do NOT add a bundle id to AppID. AppID is a pid-keyed Hashable used throughout WindowController baselines and RevealSnapshot, so changing it is risky. Instead carry an optional bundle identifier on the app MenuNode (additive, alongside representedApp), and in RadialMenuView fall back to NSWorkspace.shared.icon(forFile: bundleURL.path) when there is no running pid. Leave running-app rendering unchanged. Depends on the bundle-ID list/persistence task.
acceptance_criteria: An app node with a bundle id but no running pid renders the correct icon; running apps render exactly as before.

title: My Apps: bundle-ID list model + persistence
description: Foundation for the curated My Apps list. Define an ordered Codable model of app entries keyed by bundle identifier (store the bundle id, cache a display name, resolve the bundle URL on demand). Persist to UserDefaults as JSON under one defaults key, read fresh at summon time, mirroring the AppSortOrder and RevealStrategy pattern (a static current(from:) plus defaultsKey) so a change applies on the next summon without relaunch. Provide resolution helpers: bundle id to on-disk bundle URL via NSWorkspace.urlForApplication(withBundleIdentifier:) for icons, and bundle id to a running NSRunningApplication or pid when running via NSRunningApplication.runningApplications(withBundleIdentifier:). No UI in this task. Suggested new file CuratedApps.swift.
acceptance_criteria: An ordered list round-trips through UserDefaults; current(from:) returns empty when unset; a known bundle id resolves to a bundle URL and to a running app once launched.

title: Configurable Mouse & Trackpad activation via modifier keys (replace the unreliable three-finger trackpad click)
description:
The three-finger trackpad click isn't working out — it doesn't block the system's own gestures, so it clashes with things like Mission Control. Instead, let's make activation configurable with modifier keys.

Add two new sections to Preferences, placed right after the Accessibility section: one for the Mouse and one for the Trackpad. Each lets you configure how the menu is summoned.

Modifier keys: in both sections you can pick a combination of modifier keys — Fn, Option, Command, Control and Shift should all be available, and more than one can be selected at once (a combination).

Trackpad section:
- Only the modifier-key option is available here (no left + right click option).
- By default, Fn is selected.
- Activation works on hold only — you hold the chosen modifier(s) to bring up the menu — and no trackpad click, tap, or any other trackpad interaction is required.

Mouse section:
- A bit more flexible than the trackpad: you can choose between using a modifier-key combination OR a simultaneous left + right click to activate.
- By default, none of the modifier keys are selected.

This effectively replaces the three-finger trackpad press as the trackpad's activation method.

title: Follow-up to Bringr-93j.28: height restore is intermittent (~50/50, suspected race condition)
description:
Follow-up to Bringr-93j.28 (windows not restored to their original size in "Hide others" mode).

Even with height restoration in place, there still seems to be a problem specifically with the height, and it behaves intermittently — roughly fifty-fifty: sometimes the window's height comes back correctly, sometimes it doesn't. My hunch is another race condition around when the original height gets read and reapplied.

Expected: the original height should be restored reliably every single time, not just some of the time.

title: Second-level pie sometimes doesn't render (suspected timing/race with the window scan)
description:
Sometimes when I hover into an app to open its window sub-wheel, the second-level pie doesn't show up at all — nothing renders for that app's windows.

It seems intermittent. My hunch is that it's a timing / race issue: the app may not have finished scanning for that app's windows before it tries to draw the second-level pie, so there's nothing ready to show and it comes up empty.

Expected: every time I hover into an app that has windows, its second-level pie should reliably appear — even if the window scan needs a brief moment to finish first.

title: "Hide others" mode doesn't restore windows to their original size — they come back smaller
description:
As a user, when I use "Hide others" mode and then finish, I want every window that was moved aside to come back to exactly the size and position it had before, so that using Bringr never disturbs my screen layout.

Right now the windows aren't restored properly. I think the trick used to get them out of the way quickly is to resize or move the windows rather than truly hide them — and when they're brought back, their original size or position isn't restored correctly.

What I'm seeing: on my external 4K monitor, my Google Chrome window comes back smaller than it was before. It looks like the height is the problem, not the width — the window ends up shorter than it should be.

Expected: after "Hide others" has done its thing, every affected window should return to its exact original size and position — same width and height, in the same place — with nothing left smaller or shifted.

title: US-016: Window sub-wheel — app-aligned arcs with overflow fisheye
description:
As a user, when I open an app's window sub-wheel, I want each window slice to be as wide as an app slice and aligned outward from its parent app — instead of the windows re-dividing the whole circle — so the sub-wheel reads as an extension of the app I'm pointing at and stays easy to aim at.

Today the level-1 windows ring spans the full 360°, re-divided equally by window count (`RadialLayout.sliceSpan = 2π/itemCount`, built in `RadialNavigator.expandApp`). This changes ONLY the level-1 windows ring; the level-0 apps ring is unchanged.

## Behavior

- A window slice has the same arc width as an app slice (`2π / appCount`). Windows fan out clockwise, anchored so window 0 sits directly outside its parent app.
- windowCount == appCount: each window = one app-arc; window i sits over app (parentIndex + i) mod appCount; outer ring fully covered.
- windowCount < appCount: each window still = one full app-arc, fanning clockwise from the parent; leftover outer sectors stay empty (no slice drawn, no hit). A cursor in an empty outer sector is a no-op — the sub-wheel stays open; only the dead center or moving fully outside the wheel cancels/collapses.
- windowCount > appCount (overflow fisheye): exactly one window is focused and gets the full app-arc; all others compress equally to share the remaining (2π − appArc). Focus defaults to the first window and follows the hovered window — the focused one pops to full size and the rest re-compress, live on hover.
- Degenerate guard: when appCount == 1 (app-arc is the whole circle, no context arc to share), fall back to equal division of the circle by windowCount rather than zero-width compressed slices.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Level-1 window slices are sized to one app-arc and anchored clockwise from the parent app (windowCount <= appCount).
- [ ] windowCount < appCount leaves the uncovered outer sectors empty; a cursor there is a no-op (sub-wheel stays open), not a collapse.
- [ ] windowCount > appCount draws a fisheye: focused window at full app-arc, others compressed equally; focus defaults to the first window.
- [ ] Hovering a different window moves the fisheye focus to it (full size) and re-compresses the rest, live.
- [ ] appCount == 1 falls back to equal full-circle division (no zero-width slices).
- [ ] Rendering and hit-testing use one shared layout per ring (they cannot desync); the apps ring is visually unchanged.
- [ ] Existing window isolate-on-hover (US-011), commit (US-012), and prehighlight (US-012 AC4) still work — node index still identifies the i-th window.

## Quality Gates

- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests (incl. new variable-arc + fisheye tests)
- [ ] Build and run (`pkill -x Bringr 2>/dev/null; open build/Build/Products/Debug/Bringr.app`) and verify the sub-wheel visually for all three cases.
design:
Generalize `RadialLayout` to an explicit per-slice arc table; keep equal-slice behavior for the apps ring so it (and its tests) stay untouched. The two load-bearing invariants from the current code: (1) the same `RadialLayout` instance drives BOTH rendering (`RadialRingView` -> `RadialWedge`/`sliceCenterOffset`) and hit-testing (`RadialNavigator.region`) — they must never desync; (2) the slice `index` in `HoverRegion.slice(level:index:)` is the node's array index, used by `isolateWindow`/`commitWindow`/prehighlight — so angular reordering must change only WHERE slice i is drawn/hit, never WHICH node is slice i.

## Bringr/RadialLayout.swift

- Add `struct SliceArc: Equatable, Sendable { let start: CGFloat; let span: CGFloat }` (clockwise-from-top, y-down convention; center = start + span/2, end = start + span).
- `RadialLayout` stores `private let arcs: [SliceArc]`; `itemCount` becomes `arcs.count`.
- Keep `init(itemCount:geometry:)` building equal arcs (`arc[i] = SliceArc(start: i*span - span/2, span: span)`, span = 2π/itemCount) -> reproduces "slice 0 centered at top" exactly; apps ring byte-for-byte unchanged. Add `init(arcs:geometry:)`.
- `centerAngle/startAngle/endAngle(ofSliceAt:)` read the table. Add `span(ofSliceAt:)`; keep `sliceSpan` as `arcs.first?.span ?? 0` alias so existing tests read naturally. `ringPoint`/`sliceCenterOffset`/`normalized` unchanged.
- Rewrite `hitTest`: keep radius gate and `angle = normalized(atan2(x,-y))`; then find the arc containing `angle` via modulo-2π clockwise containment (`normalized(angle - arc.start) < arc.span`), returning the first match or `nil` when none (this is how empty sectors become no-hit, and it handles arcs that wrap past the 12 o'clock seam). Equal full-circle layouts still return exactly one slice per angle (apps ring identical to today).
- Add pure factory `static func windowRing(windowCount:appCount:parentIndex:focusedWindowIndex:geometry:) -> RadialLayout`:
  - `appArc = 2π / appCount`; `anchorStart = parentIndex*appArc - appArc/2` (parent app's leading edge, so window 0 sits over the parent).
  - Case windowCount <= appCount (focusedWindowIndex nil): slice i span = appArc, start = anchorStart + i*appArc, for i in 0..<windowCount. Leftover app-arcs get no slice.
  - Case windowCount > appCount (fisheye): focus = focusedWindowIndex ?? 0; focused slice span = appArc; the other (windowCount-1) slices share `2π - appArc` equally (`compressed = (2π - appArc)/(windowCount-1)`). Lay out clockwise in node order from anchorStart; slice i gets appArc if i == focus else compressed. Sum = 2π (contiguous full cover, focused bulges).
  - Degenerate guard: when appCount == 1 (appArc >= 2π) or `compressed` would be <= epsilon, fall back to equal division (2π/windowCount) — no zero-width slices.

## Bringr/RadialNavigator.swift

- Add stored `let layout: RadialLayout` to `RadialRing` (replaces the ad-hoc `RadialLayout(itemCount:geometry:)` both call sites build today). Computed once when the ring is (re)built and shared -> render + hit-test desync becomes structurally impossible.
- Add `private(set) var focusedWindowIndex: Int?` (nil when no overflow / no windows ring open).
- `open`: build level-0 ring layout `RadialLayout(itemCount: appNodes.count, geometry:)`; `focusedWindowIndex = nil`.
- `expandApp(at:)`: after resolving `windowNodes`, compute level-1 layout via `RadialLayout.windowRing(windowCount: windowNodes.count, appCount: appsRing.nodes.count, parentIndex: index, focusedWindowIndex: overflow ? 0 : nil, geometry: ringGeometry(forLevel: 1))`; store on the level-1 `RadialRing`; set `focusedWindowIndex` to 0 when overflow else nil. (overflow = windowNodes.count > appsRing.nodes.count.)
- `region(forOffset:)`: use `ring.layout` instead of constructing one. When the offset radius falls in the level-1 band but no arc matches (empty sector, case 2), return a no-op result (keep current `hovered`/expansion) rather than `.none`, so the wheel does NOT collapse. Distinguish this from "outside all ring bands" (which still returns `.none`).
- `updateHover` level-1 branch: call `isolateWindow(at: index)` AND new `focusWindowSlice(at: index)`.
- Add `private func focusWindowSlice(at index:)`: guard rings.count > 1; if NOT overflow, ensure focus stays nil and return (no geometric effect when every slice is already a full app-arc); guard `focusedWindowIndex != index` (idempotent); bounds-check; set `focusedWindowIndex = index`; rebuild `rings[1]` with a fresh `windowRing(...)` layout (same nodes, new focus).
- Reset `focusedWindowIndex = nil` in `clearState` and `collapse`; `expandApp` recomputes it on app re-target (so no stale focus).
- `isolateWindow`/`commitWindow`/`commitApp`/`restoreWindowIsolation`/`prehighlightRegion` cores unchanged — they index nodes by node index, which is unchanged.

## Bringr/RadialMenuView.swift

- `RadialRingView.body`: replace `let layout = RadialLayout(itemCount: ring.nodes.count, geometry: ring.geometry)` with `let layout = ring.layout`. `RadialWedge` (already samples start...end with segment count proportional to arc width), `RadialSliceLabel`, the gesture, and `RadialMenuView` need no change.

## Bringr/RadialMenuWindow.swift (RadialMenuController)

- Likely no logic change: the fisheye rebuild happens inside `updateHover` (mutates `rings[1]`), and `syncFromNavigator()` already copies `navigator.rings` into the `@Published rings` after each hover, so the view re-renders. Confirm `syncFromNavigator()` runs after every `updateHover`. Optionally publish `focusedWindowIndex` only if the view wants to style the focused slice distinctly (not required — the focused slice is the hovered one in the common case, already styled).

## Fisheye anchoring (deliberate choice)

The focused slice keeps its ordinal slot in the clockwise sweep and grows in place — do NOT recenter it over the parent app sector. This keeps the slice under the cursor when it pops, avoiding focus flicker.

## Tests (XCTest, match existing style)

BringrTests/RadialLayoutTests.swift (pure geometry, reuse assertPoint/accuracy helpers):
- equal-layout init reproduces legacy center/start/end (back-compat lock).
- case 1: appCount=4, windowCount=4, parentIndex=1 -> window i over app (1+i)%4, spans == appArc, tiles full circle.
- case 2: appCount=6, windowCount=2 -> window spans == appArc; hitTest in uncovered sector returns nil; window centers hit-test to 0/1.
- anchor at parent leading edge for several parentIndex incl. near-top (arcs wrap past 2π); hitTest round-trips across the seam.
- case 3: appCount=3, windowCount=6, focus=0 -> slice 0 span == appArc; slices 1..5 equal, sum to 2π-appArc; contiguous.
- case 3 focus moves: focus=3 -> slice 3 full, others equal/recompressed.
- hitTest round-trips every slice center under a fisheye (focus=k).
- appCount==1, windowCount=4 -> equal 2π/4 arcs (no zero-width slices).
- windowCount=1, appCount=5 -> one appArc slice at parent; hitTest outside it returns nil.

BringrTests/RadialNavigatorTests.swift (@MainActor, add a fixture with enough windows to force overflow):
- expanding an overflow app opens with focusedWindowIndex == 0 and slice 0 span == appArc.
- hovering window index 2 in overflow -> focusedWindowIndex == 2 (full arc) AND expandedWindowIndex == 2 (both effects on one hover).
- non-overflow app -> focusedWindowIndex == nil; all level-1 spans == appArc.
- focus resets to nil on app re-target and on collapse (dead zone).
- region(forOffset:) consumes the stored ring layout: focused slice center returns its index, a compressed slice center returns the right index.
- empty-sector hover (case 2) does NOT collapse: rings.count stays 2.
- prehighlight resolves the correct node index under a fisheye layout.

Existing tests reading `.sliceSpan` keep passing via the first-arc alias; no test constructs `RadialRing` directly (rings are read, not built, in navigator tests), so adding `layout` to `RadialRing` won't break call sites.

title: US-015: Cancel and guaranteed state restoration
description:
As a user, I want every way of backing out to leave my windows exactly as they were so that Bringr never strands a hidden window.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Esc, click-outside, release-in-dead-zone, and trigger-loss all cancel cleanly.
- [ ] After any cancel path, every hidden app/window is restored to its pre-summon visibility and z-order.
- [ ] If the app crashes or the menu is force-dismissed mid-reveal, no window remains hidden after relaunch (restore-on-launch safety net).
- [ ] Restoration is exercised by unit tests over the state machine and verified by build & run.

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests
- [ ] Build and run (`pkill -x Bringr 2>/dev/null; open build/Build/Products/Debug/Bringr.app`) and verify the behavior visually

title: US-014: Appearance customization
description:
As a user, I want to customize the wheel's appearance so that it fits my taste and screen.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Preferences exposes appearance options: overall size/radius, slice fill/opacity, and label visibility.
- [ ] Changes persist and apply on the next summon.
- [ ] Changing appearance never breaks slice geometry or hit-testing.
- [ ] Appearance changes are verified by build & run.

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests
- [ ] Build and run (`pkill -x Bringr 2>/dev/null; open build/Build/Products/Debug/Bringr.app`) and verify the behavior visually

title: US-011: Window-hover — isolate a single window
description:
As a user, I want hovering a window slice to leave only that window visible so that I get instant visual confirmation before committing.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Hovering a window slice applies the chosen reveal strategy to every other window of the same app, leaving only the target visible.
- [ ] Moving between window slices re-isolates the new target.
- [ ] Moving away from all window slices restores the app's other windows.
- [ ] v1 uses placeholder slice content (number/title), not a captured preview image.

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests
- [ ] Build and run (`pkill -x Bringr 2>/dev/null; open build/Build/Products/Debug/Bringr.app`) and verify the behavior visually

title: US-010: App-hover — isolate app and open window sub-wheel
description:
As a user, I want hovering an app to push the other apps out of the way and reveal that app's windows so that I can drill into the right app.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Hovering an app slice applies the chosen reveal strategy to every non-target app.
- [ ] A level-2 sub-wheel opens showing exactly the hovered app's windows from the enumeration service.
- [ ] Moving to a different app re-targets the isolation and rebuilds the sub-wheel.
- [ ] Moving back out restores the other apps.
- [ ] Nesting uses the US-005 tree (no special-cased two-level hack).

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests
- [ ] Build and run (`pkill -x Bringr 2>/dev/null; open build/Build/Products/Debug/Bringr.app`) and verify the behavior visually

title: US-009: Interaction modes — hold-to-select and click-to-stay
description:
As a user, I want to choose whether the menu stays open after I release the trigger so that it fits how I like to work.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Hold-to-select: the menu stays open while the trigger is held; releasing over a slice selects it; releasing in the dead zone cancels.
- [ ] Click-to-stay: the menu opens and remains after release; a click selects; a click outside or Esc cancels.
- [ ] The active mode is configurable and persisted.
- [ ] Both modes drive the same selection/cancel paths (no divergent behavior beyond open/persist semantics).
- [ ] The mode state machine is unit-tested.

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests
- [ ] Build and run (`pkill -x Bringr 2>/dev/null; open build/Build/Products/Debug/Bringr.app`) and verify the behavior visually

title: US-007: Activation — simultaneous left+right mouse click
description:
As a mouse user, I want pressing left and right buttons together to summon the menu so that I can trigger it without the keyboard.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A global event tap detects left and right button presses occurring within a small configurable threshold as a single "summon" trigger.
- [ ] Normal single left/right clicks pass through unaffected when the combo is not detected.
- [ ] The menu summons at the current cursor position.
- [ ] The trigger does not leak/duplicate events to the focused app.
- [ ] Threshold and detection logic are unit-tested in isolation from the live event stream.

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests
- [ ] Build and run (`pkill -x Bringr 2>/dev/null; open build/Build/Products/Debug/Bringr.app`) and verify the behavior visually

title: US-008: Activation — three-finger trackpad press
description:
As a trackpad user, I want a three-finger press to summon the menu so that I can trigger it without a mouse or keyboard.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A three-finger trackpad press summons the menu at the cursor.
- [ ] Two-finger and single-finger gestures are unaffected.
- [ ] If the required multitouch detection is unavailable on the host, the app fails safe (no crash) and the limitation is logged/surfaced.
- [ ] Detectable, host-independent parts of the gesture logic are unit-tested; end-to-end behavior is verified by build & run.

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests
- [ ] Build and run (`pkill -x Bringr 2>/dev/null; open build/Build/Products/Debug/Bringr.app`) and verify the behavior visually

title: Extend the "hide everything else" checkbox to app selection, without minimizing windows
description:
Follow-up to Bringr-93j.27 (the "hide everything else after a selection" checkbox). Today that checkbox only does something when you select a specific window — it should also apply when you select an app. And the way it hides things needs to change: hiding all but one window of an app would mean minimizing the app's other windows, which is slow and unpleasant. Let's avoid minimizing altogether.

With the checkbox checked, there are two cases:

1. You select an app (not a specific window): hide all the OTHER applications' windows, and keep all of the selected app's windows on screen. Don't minimize anything — every window of the chosen app stays.

2. You select a specific window: same as above — hide all the other applications' windows and keep all of the selected app's windows on screen — but additionally activate the window you picked (bring it to the front / focus it).

The key point: never minimize the chosen app's other windows just to surface one. Hiding only ever applies to other apps; within the selected app, all windows stay visible and the chosen one (if any) is simply activated.
notes: Observed side effect: when an app's window has been hidden (not minimized — actually hidden, e.g. by this feature's "hide other apps" behaviour or via Hide / Cmd-H), that window is not collected the next time you open the pie menu, so it disappears from the switcher. The fix for surfacing those again lives in the collection settings — see Bringr-93j.50, which adds a "collect hidden windows" checkbox.

title: Recent-use sort gets polluted by previewing — hovering a window changes the Cmd+Tab order
description:
When the sort order is set to "by recent use" (for apps or for windows), just previewing a window in the menu changes the system's recent-use order (the Cmd+Tab order). Previewing brings the window forward to show it, and macOS treats that as actually using the window, so it jumps to the front of the recent list.

The side effect: the very next time I open the pie menu, the order is unpredictable — simply hovering over something I didn't actually want to pick has already reshuffled the recent-use ordering. So just browsing the menu pollutes the order it's based on.

What I'd like: previewing / hovering a window should not change the system's recent-use (Cmd+Tab) order. Only an actual committed selection should count as "using" a window. Please come up with a way to bypass or work around this so that hovering for a preview leaves the recent-use order untouched.

Low priority.

title: Show the Dock icon only while the Preferences window is open
description:
Right now Bringr lives only in the menu bar, so when you open Preferences the window appears but the app has no Dock icon at all — which feels a bit off for a normal window.

Let's make the Dock icon appear while the Preferences window is open, and disappear again once it's closed:
- Preferences open → the app shows a Dock icon and behaves like a normal windowed app.
- Preferences closed → no Dock icon, back to being menu-bar-only exactly as it is today.

Low priority.

title: Preferences for app and window sort order (default apps to Cmd+Tab order; windows by recent-use or static)
description:
I'd like to add a couple more preferences, both about sorting.

1. App sort order. A setting that controls how the apps are arranged in the pie. You can keep whatever sorting we do today as one of the options, but the default should match the Cmd+Tab order — the exact same sequence, laid out starting from twelve o'clock (the top) and going around the wheel.

2. Window sort order. A second setting that controls how an app's windows are arranged in the second-level pie. Two options:
- By recent use — windows ordered by how recently they were used.
- Static / fixed position — each window keeps a stable spot: the window that was open first stays first, the second stays second, and so on, so their positions don't jump around.

Important: keep this simple, don't over-engineer it. I don't want us writing or listening to events to track when windows open or how recently they're used and remembering their positions ourselves. These orderings should come straight from what macOS already gives us (e.g. the Cmd+Tab ordering and the system's own window order). If macOS doesn't actually expose what's needed for one of these options, don't build elaborate tracking to fake it — just update this task to note that it wasn't possible to implement, and leave it at that.

title: Update the README to reflect the app's current features and behavior
description: Bring the README up to date with where Bringr is now — its current features, how it works, and any recent changes — so it accurately reflects the app instead of being out of date.

title: Multi-monitor: only list apps and windows for the screen the menu was opened on
description:
With more than one monitor connected, the pie menu should only show the apps and windows that live on the screen where it was summoned — the currently selected screen — rather than everything across all the screens.

So if I open the menu on one monitor, I should only see the apps and windows that are on that monitor; anything sitting on my other screens shouldn't appear in the list.

title: Optional cursor lock that keeps the pointer on the open app and its windows in the second-level pie
description:
As a user, I want an optional setting that locks my cursor inside the second-level pie once it opens, so that I can't accidentally slide the pointer off the app and its windows while I'm choosing one.

This is an optional setting (off unless I turn it on). When it's on:

- The moment I hover into the second level of the pie (an app's window sub-wheel), the cursor becomes locked.
- While locked, I can only move the cursor among that app's window arcs (the second-level options) and the arc of the app that opened them. I can't move the pointer anywhere else on screen.
- When I move the cursor back onto the app arc I started from (the app that opened this second level), the lock releases and I can move around freely again — for example, back out to the other apps in the first-level pie.

When the setting is off, the cursor moves freely as it does today.

title: Preferences checkbox: hide everything else on screen after a selection is committed
description:
As a user, I want an option that clears everything else off the screen once I've picked something, so that I'm left with only what I selected and an uncluttered screen.

This is a checkbox in Preferences. When it's turned on:

- If I select an app, all the other apps on screen get hidden, leaving only the app I chose.
- If I select a specific window, all the other windows on screen get hidden, leaving only that one window.

When I release the mouse while hovering on an app itself — not drilling into one of its specific windows, just the app — and this checkbox is enabled, then by default all the other windows should be hidden as well, so committing at the app level also clears everything else away.

When the checkbox is off, selecting something behaves as it does today and nothing extra is hidden.

title: Refine the Preferences window and make it scrollable so everything fits
description:
The Preferences window is getting cramped — there's no longer enough room to comfortably show everything that's in it.

Refine the Preferences window and make its content scrollable, so that as more settings get added everything stays reachable instead of being cut off or squeezed into too little space.

title: "Hide others" mode feels slow at the window level because it minimizes other windows — find a faster way
description:
In "Hide others" mode, hovering over an app works well. But once you start hovering over the individual windows of a specific app, the mode reacts by minimizing all the other apps' windows to get them out of the way.

The problem is that minimizing windows in macOS is animated and quite slow, so doing it for everything makes the whole experience feel sluggish.

We should come up with a different way to get the other windows out of the way at the window level, without actually minimizing them. If genuinely hiding them turns out not to be possible, one idea is to simply move those windows instead — e.g. shift their position off to the side or out of view — so the result looks the same but happens instantly.

title: Three-finger click should suppress other trackpad gestures while it's used to open the menu
description:
Rework the MacBook trackpad behaviour so that when the menu is summoned with a three-finger click, no other trackpad gestures are allowed to fire at the same time.

The concern: many Mac users have Mission Control set to a three-finger gesture, so we want to make sure that using three fingers to open Bringr never accidentally triggers anything else.

In practice this probably won't clash, because Mission Control reacts to a three-finger swipe — it does not require an actual physical trackpad press or click, which is what Bringr relies on. So a real three-finger press shouldn't set Mission Control off.

Even so, while a three-finger click is being used to bring up Bringr's menu, the app should hold back any other gesture the system might otherwise read from those three fingers, so nothing else happens behind the menu.

I'm not sure whether this is actually possible — please look into it first, and if it is, implement it.

title: Three-finger gesture fires on tap/rest instead of requiring an actual click
description:
The three-finger trackpad activation (US-008, Bringr-93j.8) summons the menu as soon as three fingers land/rest on the trackpad — a light tap or even resting three fingers triggers it. It should fire only when the user performs an actual physical click (press) with three fingers, so accidental three-finger touches do not summon the wheel.

## Expected
Summon only on a real three-finger click (the trackpad physically clicks / force-click), not on a three-finger tap or rest.

## Actual
Any frame where exactly three fingers are present triggers a press.

## Suspected cause
ThreeFingerPressDetector.handle(fingerCount:) (Bringr/ThreeFingerActivation.swift:41) returns .press purely on the rising edge to requiredFingerCount fingers. It consumes only the finger COUNT (numTouches) from the MultitouchSupport contact callback (lines 81-89), which reports touch presence — not click or pressure state. There is no button/click gating, so a three-finger rest/tap is indistinguishable from a three-finger click.

## Likely fix
Gate the press on an actual click: require a physical trackpad click (leftMouseDown via NSEvent, or force-click pressure) while exactly three fingers are present, rather than firing on finger landing alone. Preserve the existing release semantics that hold-to-select uses to commit (US-009).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Three-finger activation fires only on a physical three-finger click, not on a tap or rest.
- [ ] A light three-finger tap or resting three fingers never summons the menu.
- [ ] The release signal for hold-to-select still works after a real three-finger click.
- [ ] One- and two-finger gestures still pass through untouched.
- [ ] Verified by build & run on a trackpad.

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests
- [ ] Build and run (`pkill -x Bringr 2>/dev/null; open build/Build/Products/Debug/Bringr.app`) and verify the behavior visually

title: Switching interaction-mode setting kills hover feedback in the pie menu
description:
After changing the interaction-mode / mouse setting in Preferences ("When summoned:" picker, Bringr/PreferencesView.swift:36 — hold-to-select vs click-to-stay / mouse-release behaviour, US-009 Bringr-93j.9), summoning the pie menu and moving the cursor over slices produces no hover reaction: slices do not highlight and app/window isolation (US-010 / US-011) never triggers.

## Repro
1. Open Preferences and switch the interaction mode to the click/release-based mode (click-to-stay).
2. Summon the pie menu.
3. Move the cursor over apps/windows.
Expected: the hovered slice emphasises and the app/window isolates.
Actual: nothing happens — hover never updates.

## Suspected cause
Hover is driven solely by a global event monitor: RadialMenuWindow.startHoverTracking() installs NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents (Bringr/RadialMenuWindow.swift:201), whose callback calls updateHover (line 213). The code comment (lines 66-67) assumes this "works in both interaction modes," but a global monitor does NOT fire for events delivered to your own application. In the click-based mode the overlay/app receives the mouse-moved events itself, so the global monitor never fires and updateHover is never called. In hold-to-select the originating app stays active during the held chord, so the events are global and the monitor works — which is why the break only appears after switching the setting.

## Likely fix
Also install a local monitor (NSEvent.addLocalMonitorForEvents) and/or a tracking area / accept mouse-moved on the overlay window, so hover updates regardless of which app owns the events. Verify the overlay's activation/key behaviour in click-to-stay vs hold-to-select.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Hover highlighting and app/window isolation work in BOTH interaction modes (hold-to-select and click-to-stay).
- [ ] Switching the interaction-mode setting and re-summoning never loses hover feedback.
- [ ] A test or harness exercises hover updates under both modes (or the monitor wiring is covered) so the regression is caught.
- [ ] Verified by build & run in each mode.

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests
- [ ] Build and run (`pkill -x Bringr 2>/dev/null; open build/Build/Products/Debug/Bringr.app`) and verify the behavior visually

title: Release & distribution infrastructure (Bringr)
description:
Umbrella epic for shipping Bringr to users: tag-driven CI/CD, in-app auto-updates, and a public documentation website. Modeled on the setup in ~/Projects/ClipSlop.

Children:
- GitHub Actions: build + tag-driven release (and docs/appcast deploy).
- Sparkle: in-app automatic updates (key pair, Info.plist keys, appcast).
- Docusaurus: documentation website deployed to GitHub Pages.

The GitHub Actions child depends on both the Sparkle child (signs the DMG, updates appcast) and the Docusaurus child (docs-deploy workflow needs the site).

title: Initialize Sparkle for automatic updates in Bringr
description:
Integrate Sparkle 2 auto-updates the way ClipSlop does, so Bringr can update itself outside the App Store. This is the in-app + signing-key half; the release workflow (Bringr-zkc) consumes it to sign DMGs and publish the appcast.

HOW CLIPSLOP DOES IT:
- Sparkle SPM package (github.com/sparkle-project/Sparkle, from 2.6.0).
- A SparkleUpdater service wraps SPUStandardUpdaterController(startingUpdater: true), bails out unless Info.plist has SUFeedURL, and exposes checkForUpdates(); a Check for Updates menu item and an About-window button call it.
- Info.plist keys: SUFeedURL = https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mekedron/clipslop/main/appcast.xml and SUPublicEDKey = the Ed25519 public key.
- An Ed25519 key pair is generated with Sparkle generate_keys; the public key goes in Info.plist, the private key is stored as the GitHub secret SPARKLE_PRIVATE_KEY.
- On each tagged release, the workflow runs sign_update on the DMG and commits an updated appcast.xml to main.

ADAPTATION FOR BRINGR:
- Add Sparkle via Xcode SPM (Bringr is an .xcodeproj, not Package.swift). Check entitlements/sandbox implications for a status-bar app.
- Generate a NEW Bringr-specific Ed25519 key pair. Put SUPublicEDKey in Bringr Info.plist; set SUFeedURL to https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mekedron/Bringr/main/appcast.xml; store the private key as the SPARKLE_PRIVATE_KEY GitHub secret; commit an initial appcast.xml at repo root.
- Add a SparkleUpdater service and a Check for Updates item to Bringr menu-bar menu / About window.

DEPENDENCY: the GitHub Actions release workflow (Bringr-zkc) signs the DMG and updates appcast.xml, so it depends on this task (key pair, Info.plist keys, initial appcast must exist first).
acceptance_criteria: Sparkle package added; SparkleUpdater wired with a working Check for Updates action; Ed25519 key pair generated; SUPublicEDKey + SUFeedURL set in Info.plist; SPARKLE_PRIVATE_KEY stored as a GitHub secret; initial appcast.xml committed.

title: Add Launch at Login option to Bringr
description:
Let users have Bringr start automatically at login, the way ClipSlop does. ClipSlop uses the LaunchAtLogin-Modern SPM package (github.com/sindresorhus/LaunchAtLogin-Modern, from 1.0.0), which wraps SMAppService (no legacy login-item helper bundle). It is exposed in the UI simply as LaunchAtLogin.Toggle(...) inside GeneralSettingsView, and listed as a credit in the About view.

ADAPTATION FOR BRINGR:
- Bringr is an Xcode project (not a Package.swift SPM project), so add the package via Xcode > File > Add Package Dependencies using URL https://github.com/sindresorhus/LaunchAtLogin-Modern and link it to the Bringr target.
- Bringr is status-bar-only (LSUIElement) with About and Preferences windows; add a Launch at login toggle to the Preferences window (or the menu-bar menu).
- Verify SMAppService registration works for a non-sandboxed status-bar app and persists across logout/reboot.
acceptance_criteria: A Launch at login toggle exists in Bringr Preferences; enabling it registers Bringr as a login item via SMAppService and it relaunches after logout/reboot; disabling it unregisters.

title: Build Docusaurus documentation website for Bringr
description:
Bringr has a docs/ folder but no published documentation website. ClipSlop ships a Docusaurus site under docs-site/ that is built and deployed to GitHub Pages by its deploy-docs.yml / test-docs-build.yml workflows. To reuse those workflows for Bringr (see the GitHub Actions port task), Bringr needs an equivalent Docusaurus site first.

SCOPE:
- Scaffold a Docusaurus site under docs-site/ (Node 20, npm), mirroring the ClipSlop layout at ~/Projects/ClipSlop/docs-site for reference.
- Wire existing Bringr docs/ content into the site and configure it for GitHub Pages (correct baseUrl/url for the mekedron/Bringr repo).
- Ensure npm ci and npm run build succeed locally so the CI workflows can build and deploy it.

This task unblocks the docs-deploy and docs-test workflows in the GitHub Actions port.
acceptance_criteria: A docs-site/ Docusaurus project exists; npm ci and npm run build succeed; site is configured for GitHub Pages deployment for the mekedron/Bringr repository.

title: Port ClipSlop GitHub Actions (CI + tag-driven release) to Bringr
description:
Replicate the GitHub Actions setup from ~/Projects/ClipSlop for Bringr. ClipSlop has three workflows under .github/workflows: (1) release.yml, (2) deploy-docs.yml, (3) test-docs-build.yml. Bringr has no .github yet. Remote already exists at git@github.com:mekedron/Bringr.git.

HOW CLIPSLOP WORKS:
- release.yml: triggered on push of a v*.*.* tag. Sets up Xcode (maxim-lobanov/setup-xcode), builds a universal binary, assembles a .app bundle, sets version from the tag, ad-hoc codesigns, builds a UDZO DMG, auto-generates release notes by grouping conventional-commit subjects (feat/fix/other) since the previous tag, then publishes a GitHub Release via softprops/action-gh-release. It also signs the DMG for Sparkle, commits an updated appcast.xml to main, and updates a Homebrew cask in a separate tap repo.
- deploy-docs.yml: on push to main touching docs, builds the Docusaurus docs-site and deploys to GitHub Pages.
- test-docs-build.yml: on PRs touching docs, builds the docs-site without deploying.

KEY ADAPTATIONS FOR BRINGR:
- ClipSlop is a Swift Package Manager project (swift build --arch arm64 --arch x86_64 + manual .app assembly). Bringr is an XCODE project: build with xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr (archive + export, or a Release build), which already emits a .app bundle, so the manual bundle-assembly steps differ.
- Bringr is a status-bar-only app (LSUIElement); keep that Info.plist key.
- Sparkle auto-update (appcast.xml signing) and the Homebrew cask update are OPTIONAL: Bringr has no Sparkle integration, no appcast.xml, and no Homebrew tap yet. Either omit these steps initially or set them up first (requires SPARKLE_PRIVATE_KEY and HOMEBREW_TAP_TOKEN secrets).
- The two docs workflows REQUIRE a Docusaurus docs-site, which Bringr does not have yet (see the blocking docs-website task).
acceptance_criteria: Three workflows live under Bringr/.github/workflows, adapted to the Xcode build. Pushing a v*.*.* tag produces an ad-hoc-signed .app and DMG attached to an auto-generated GitHub Release. The docs workflows build and deploy the Docusaurus site to GitHub Pages.

title: US-013: Reveal-strategy setting
description:
As a user, I want to choose how "other" windows/apps get out of the way so that the behavior matches my preference.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Preferences offers three reveal strategies: raise-target-to-front, hide-others, and dim-others.
- [ ] The chosen strategy applies to both the app-hover (US-010) and window-hover (US-011) levels.
- [ ] All three strategies are fully implemented and selectable.
- [ ] The setting is persisted and takes effect on the next summon without relaunch.
- [ ] A sensible default is set and documented.

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests
- [ ] Build and run (`pkill -x Bringr 2>/dev/null; open build/Build/Products/Debug/Bringr.app`) and verify the behavior visually

title: Previewed windows aren't restored to their original stacking order after selecting a different window
description:
A very minor cosmetic bug. When previewing apps/windows, hovering brings a window up to the front so you can see it. But when you then select a different window, the windows you previewed along the way aren't put back to their previous place in the stacking order — they stay raised instead of returning to where they were.

The important part already works correctly: the window/app you actually choose ends up on top of everything else. This is only about the other windows you happened to hover over not being returned to their original order behind it.

Not important — super low priority — but if there's an easy way to restore the previewed windows to their original positions afterwards, it'd be a nice touch.
notes: Don't over-engineer this — no crazy hacks. If restoring the original stacking order turns out to carry any real risk (e.g. it could disturb the chosen window ending up on top, or otherwise make things worse), then leave the current behaviour intact and don't force a fix.

title: Optional trackpad haptic feedback when hovering pie items (trackpad-only)
description:
A nice-to-have feel feature: optional haptic feedback through the MacBook's trackpad. When there's no external mouse connected — so you're on the built-in trackpad — the trackpad's haptic engine gives a little tactile tap as you hover from one item to the next in the pie menu, so you can actually feel the selection moving.

Add a checkbox in Preferences to turn this on or off, and maybe a slider to adjust the strength/intensity of the haptic.

This naturally belongs with the Trackpad settings, and only applies when using the trackpad (no external mouse connected) — with a mouse there's no haptic engine to drive, so it simply doesn't apply.

Super low priority — purely polish.
